question: Walk me through what makes something necessarily true versus only sometimes true. Use a concrete example for each.
english_query: Walk me through what makes something necessarily true versus only sometimes true. Use a concrete example for each.
ugf_query: walk through what makes a thing have to be true no matter what versus only sometimes being true. use a clear case for each.
difficulty_note: UGF lacks 'necessary', 'possible', 'contingent'. Must paraphrase via 'must be true no matter what' / 'could be true some way'. Tests whether the modal/non-modal distinction comes through under paraphrase.

question: Could the laws of physics have been different than they are? Why or why not? What hangs on the answer?
english_query: Could the laws of physics have been different than they are? Why or why not? What hangs on the answer?
ugf_query: could the rules that the world goes by have been different than they are? why or why not? what hangs on the answer?
difficulty_note: Tests modal claim about physical law contingency. Requires the model to grasp 'could have been otherwise' as a meaningful claim distinct from 'are not'.

question: Some thinkers say there are many ways the world could have been, and call these 'possible worlds'. What does this mean and what work does the idea do?
english_query: Some thinkers say there are many ways the world could have been, and call these 'possible worlds'. What does this mean and what work does the idea do?
ugf_query: some thinkers say there are many ways the world could have been, and call these 'ways the world might be'. what does this mean and what work does the idea do?
difficulty_note: Possible-worlds machinery is hard to render in UGF without tedious circumlocution. Tests whether the model can carry the modal-realism / model-theory machinery via paraphrase.

question: Distinguish between something being permitted, required, and going above and beyond what is required. Give an example of each.
english_query: Distinguish between something being permitted, required, and going above and beyond what is required. Give an example of each.
ugf_query: explain the difference between something being okay to do, having to do, and going beyond what you have to do. give a case of each.
difficulty_note: Tests the supererogation/obligation/permission distinction. UGF lacks 'obligation', 'permitted', 'supererogation'. Must paraphrase. Whether the three-way distinction survives paraphrase is the test.

question: If two duties pull you in opposite directions, what do you do? Walk through how to think about a case.
english_query: If two duties pull you in opposite directions, what do you do? Walk through how to think about a case.
ugf_query: if you have to do two things that pull you in opposite ways, what do you do? walk through how to think about a case.
difficulty_note: Moral dilemma reasoning. Tests the model's ability to recognize residual obligation, weigh competing duties, distinguish 'all-things-considered ought' from 'prima facie ought'. None of this jargon is available in UGF.

question: Why might it be wrong to break a promise even when keeping it leads to a worse outcome overall?
english_query: Why might it be wrong to break a promise even when keeping it leads to a worse outcome overall?
ugf_query: why might it be wrong to break a promise even when keeping it leads to a worse end for everyone overall?
difficulty_note: Tests the deontology-vs-consequentialism distinction. Requires the model to articulate why duties matter beyond outcomes.

question: Some say minds rest on brains in a special way: same brain state, same mind state. Walk through what this claim means and why it is hard to spell out.
english_query: Some say minds rest on brains in a special way: same brain state, same mind state. Walk through what this claim means and why it is hard to spell out.
ugf_query: some say minds rest on brains in a special way: same brain state, same mind state. walk through what this idea means and why it is hard to spell out clearly.
difficulty_note: Tests supervenience as 'no difference in X without difference in Y'. UGF can express the biconditional via 'same...same' but capturing the ASYMMETRY (mind on brain, not brain on mind) is harder.

question: Can two worlds be exactly alike in all physical ways but different in their moral facts? Why or why not?
english_query: Can two worlds be exactly alike in all physical ways but different in their moral facts? Why or why not?
ugf_query: can two worlds be the same in all body ways but different in their right-and-wrong facts? why or why not?
difficulty_note: Classic moral-supervenience question. Requires the model to articulate that moral facts can't float free of physical facts (or to defend the contrary). Tests whether the model has the supervenience structure even without the word.

question: If beauty rests on physical features of an object, what does that imply about whether there could be a 'beauty atom' separable from physical features?
english_query: If beauty rests on physical features of an object, what does that imply about whether there could be a 'beauty atom' separable from physical features?
ugf_query: if beauty rests on body parts of a thing, what does that say about whether there could be a 'beauty bit' that is not the body parts?
difficulty_note: Tests the same supervenience structure for aesthetics. Whether the model treats 'rests on' as the asymmetric dependence relation is the diagnostic.

question: Distinguish between using a word and talking about a word. Use 'cat' as your example.
english_query: Distinguish between using a word and talking about a word. Use 'cat' as your example.
ugf_query: explain the difference between using a word and talking about a word. use 'cat' as your case.
difficulty_note: Use/mention distinction. UGF has 'word', 'name', 'thing'. The distinction can be made via 'the word X' vs 'X' but it requires careful staging.

question: 'Snow' has four letters. Snow is white. Why are these two different kinds of claim and what difference does it make?
english_query: 'Snow' has four letters. Snow is white. Why are these two different kinds of claim and what difference does it make?
ugf_query: 'snow' has four letters. snow is white. why are these two different kinds of claim and what difference does it make?
difficulty_note: Concrete use/mention case. The two sentences differ only in whether 'snow' is the word or the stuff. Tests whether the model grasps the meta-linguistic shift.

question: When someone says 'Bob said "I am hungry"', what's the difference between Bob's word 'I' and the speaker's word 'I'?
english_query: When someone says 'Bob said "I am hungry"', what's the difference between Bob's word 'I' and the speaker's word 'I'?
ugf_query: when a person says 'Bob said "I am hungry"', what is the difference between Bob's word 'I' and the talker's word 'I'?
difficulty_note: Tests indirect quotation and indexical context-shifting. Hard because 'I' refers to different people in the two contexts; UGF lacks 'indexical' but the structure is graspable.

question: Lois believes Superman flies. Lois does not believe Clark flies. But Superman is Clark. How can both her beliefs be coherent?
english_query: Lois believes Superman flies. Lois does not believe Clark flies. But Superman is Clark. How can both her beliefs be coherent?
ugf_query: Lois thinks Superman flies. Lois does not think Clark flies. But Superman is Clark. how can both her thoughts hold together?
difficulty_note: Classic Frege-Russell puzzle about substitutivity in belief contexts. Tests whether the model has the intensional/extensional distinction even without the words.

question: Why can't we always swap one true description of a thing for another in a sentence? Walk through a case where the swap fails.
english_query: Why can't we always swap one true description of a thing for another in a sentence? Walk through a case where the swap fails.
ugf_query: why can we not always trade one true name for a thing for another true name for the same thing in a sentence? walk through a case where the trade fails.
difficulty_note: Tests substitutivity failure under propositional attitudes. The standard examples (belief, knowledge, modal) all rely on words that aren't in UGF.

question: If 'the morning star' and 'the evening star' name the same body in the sky, why is 'the morning star is the evening star' news worth saying?
english_query: If 'the morning star' and 'the evening star' name the same body in the sky, why is 'the morning star is the evening star' news worth saying?
ugf_query: if 'the morning star' and 'the evening star' name the same body in the sky, why is 'the morning star is the evening star' news worth saying?
difficulty_note: Frege's puzzle. Tests sense/reference distinction. UGF has 'name' and 'body' but no clean way to distinguish sense from reference.

question: 'This sentence is false.' What's the problem? What does the problem teach us about truth or sentences?
english_query: 'This sentence is false.' What's the problem? What does the problem teach us about truth or sentences?
ugf_query: 'this line of words is not true.' what is the problem? what does the problem teach us about being true or about lines of words?
difficulty_note: Liar paradox. Tests recognition of the contradiction (if true then false; if false then true) and what to make of it. UGF can carry this if the model handles the self-reference structure.

question: Can a list of all lists that don't include themselves include itself? Walk through what this asks and why it matters.
english_query: Can a list of all lists that don't include themselves include itself? Walk through what this asks and why it matters.
ugf_query: can a list of all lists that do not have themselves on them have itself on it? walk through what this asks and why it is important.
difficulty_note: Russell's paradox in folk-set form. UGF has 'list' and 'have on it'. Tests whether the model can trace the contradiction (if it's on, it shouldn't be; if it isn't, it should be).

question: Some words describe themselves (like 'short' is short); some don't (like 'long' is not long). Is 'words that don't describe themselves' a word that describes itself?
english_query: Some words describe themselves (like 'short' is short); some don't (like 'long' is not long). Is 'words that don't describe themselves' a word that describes itself?
ugf_query: some words stand for themselves (like 'short' is short); some do not (like 'long' is not long). is 'words that do not stand for themselves' a word that stands for itself?
difficulty_note: Grelling's paradox. Same structure as Russell. Tests whether the model can apply the contradiction-trace template to a different surface.

question: One grain of sand isn't a heap. Adding one grain to a non-heap doesn't make a heap. So a heap is impossible. What's wrong with this argument?
english_query: One grain of sand isn't a heap. Adding one grain to a non-heap doesn't make a heap. So a heap is impossible. What's wrong with this argument?
ugf_query: one little bit of sand is not a pile. putting one little bit on a not-pile does not make a pile. so a pile is not possible. what is wrong with this thinking?
difficulty_note: Sorites paradox. Tests recognition that one of the premises must fail (or the conclusion is acceptable). UGF can carry the structure.

question: Where exactly does someone become tall? Defend your answer or argue there is no exact answer.
english_query: Where exactly does someone become tall? Defend your answer or argue there is no exact answer.
ugf_query: where exactly does a person become tall? say what you think and why, or say there is no exact answer.
difficulty_note: Vagueness about gradable predicates. Tests sharp-cutoff vs no-cutoff vs degree-theory positions. UGF handles 'tall' fine but the meta-linguistic question is harder.

question: 'Bald' has fuzzy edges. Does that mean some sentences using 'bald' have no clear true-or-false value? Or what?
english_query: 'Bald' has fuzzy edges. Does that mean some sentences using 'bald' have no clear true-or-false value? Or what?
ugf_query: 'no hair' has fuzzy edges. does that mean some lines of words using 'no hair' have no clear true-or-not-true value? or what?
difficulty_note: Tests the truth-value-gap option for vague predicates. Requires the model to reason about whether truth-value can be partial.

question: What would it take for a glass to be fragile? Walk through what 'fragile' commits us to.
english_query: What would it take for a glass to be fragile? Walk through what 'fragile' commits us to.
ugf_query: what would it take for a cup to break easily? walk through what 'breaks easily' says about the cup.
difficulty_note: Tests dispositional analysis: fragility is a counterfactual property (would break IF struck). UGF can express 'would break if hit' so the structure is available.

question: If I had been kinder, things would have gone better. What does 'would have' mean here? How is it different from saying 'they did go better'?
english_query: If I had been kinder, things would have gone better. What does 'would have' mean here? How is it different from saying 'they did go better'?
ugf_query: if I had been kinder, things would have gone better. what does 'would have' mean here? how is it different from saying 'they did go better'?
difficulty_note: Counterfactual conditional vs indicative. UGF can carry 'would have' / 'did' but the distinction is subtle.

question: Salt dissolves in water — even when there's no water around. What kind of fact about the salt is this?
english_query: Salt dissolves in water — even when there's no water around. What kind of fact about the salt is this?
ugf_query: salt goes into water — even when there is no water around. what kind of fact about the salt is this?
difficulty_note: Dispositional fact, present tense, active even when conditions don't obtain. Tests whether the model grasps standing dispositions.

question: Are numbers things in the world? If yes, where? If no, what are they?
english_query: Are numbers things in the world? If yes, where? If no, what are they?
ugf_query: are numbers things in the world? if yes, where? if no, what are they?
difficulty_note: Platonism vs nominalism. UGF has 'number', 'thing', 'in the world' so the question is statable. Tests the abstract/concrete distinction.

question: Two leaves are both green. What is 'green' — a thing both have, an idea, or what?
english_query: Two leaves are both green. What is 'green' — a thing both have, an idea, or what?
ugf_query: two leaves are both green. what is 'green' — a thing both have, an idea, or what?
difficulty_note: Universals vs particulars. UGF lacks 'universal'. Must reason about whether 'green' is a thing, an idea, a name, or something else.

question: When 'CAT' is on a sign, is the word on the sign the same word as 'CAT' on a different sign? In what sense yes, in what sense no?
english_query: When 'CAT' is on a sign, is the word on the sign the same word as 'CAT' on a different sign? In what sense yes, in what sense no?
ugf_query: when 'CAT' is on a sign, is the word on the sign the same word as 'CAT' on a different sign? in what way yes, in what way no?
difficulty_note: Type/token distinction. UGF lacks 'type', 'token'. Must paraphrase via 'kind of word' vs 'one example of it'. Tests whether the structure survives.

question: Why is there something it's like to taste coffee? Could a perfect description of the brain capture this?
english_query: Why is there something it's like to taste coffee? Could a perfect description of the brain capture this?
ugf_query: why is there something it feels like to taste coffee? could a perfect telling of the brain catch this?
difficulty_note: The hard problem of consciousness. UGF lacks 'qualia', 'phenomenal'. Tests whether the explanatory-gap intuition can be expressed.

question: Imagine someone who knows everything physical about color but has only seen black and white. When they first see red, do they learn something new?
english_query: Imagine someone who knows everything physical about color but has only seen black and white. When they first see red, do they learn something new?
ugf_query: think of someone who knows every body fact about color but has only seen black and white. when they first see red, do they learn something new?
difficulty_note: Mary's room. Tests whether the model grasps the knowledge-argument intuition: that 'what it's like' is not captured by physical knowledge alone.

question: Could two people see colors flipped from each other and never know? What follows?
english_query: Could two people see colors flipped from each other and never know? What follows?
ugf_query: could two people see colors flipped from each other and never know? what follows?
difficulty_note: Inverted qualia. Tests whether the model grasps the under-determination of phenomenal experience by behavior.
